title: Memory Addressing in ARM
summary: Load/store architecture, LDR/STR, addressing modes, stack operations, and memory layout.
content_html:
Memory Addressing in ARM Assembly

ARM is a load/store architecture: arithmetic and logic operations work only on data in registers. To process data stored in memory, you must first load it into a register, operate on it, then store the result back. This is a defining characteristic of RISC architectures and simplifies the processor pipeline.

LDR — Load Register

The LDR instruction loads a 32-bit word from memory into a register:

  • LDR R0, [R1] — load the word at the address stored in R1 into R0. R1 is used as a base register.
  • The square brackets [ ] denote that R1 holds an address, not a value — it is a memory dereference, similar to a pointer in C.

LDR variants for smaller data sizes:

  • LDRB — load a single byte (8 bits), zero-extend to 32 bits
  • LDRSB — load a signed byte, sign-extend to 32 bits
  • LDRH — load a halfword (16 bits), zero-extend
  • LDRSH — load a signed halfword, sign-extend

STR — Store Register

The STR instruction stores a register’s value to memory:

  • STR R0, [R1] — store the value in R0 to the memory address in R1

STR variants:

  • STRB — store only the lowest byte of the register
  • STRH — store only the lowest halfword

Addressing Modes

ARM provides several addressing modes for memory access, all using a base register plus an optional offset:

  1. Register indirect (no offset): [Rn] — address is exactly the value in Rn. Example: LDR R0, [R3]
  2. Offset addressing: [Rn, #offset] — address = Rn + offset. The base register is not modified. Example: LDR R0, [R1, #8] loads from address R1+8.
  3. Pre-indexed (write-back): [Rn, #offset]! — address = Rn + offset, and then Rn is updated to Rn + offset. The ! indicates write-back. Useful for advancing a pointer through an array.
  4. Post-indexed: [Rn], #offset — address = Rn (the current value), then Rn += offset. The memory access uses the original Rn, then Rn is updated. Ideal for iterating through arrays.
  5. Register offset: [Rn, Rm] or [Rn, Rm, LSL #n] — address = Rn + Rm (optionally shifted). Useful for array indexing.

The Stack

The stack is a region of memory used for temporary storage. It operates as a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure. In ARM:

  • SP (R13) points to the top of the stack.
  • The stack grows downward — pushing data decreases SP; popping increases SP.
  • PUSH {Rn, ...} stores registers onto the stack: decrements SP by 4 for each register and stores them.
  • POP {Rn, ...} restores registers from the stack: loads them and increments SP.

Example: PUSH {R4, R5, LR} saves R4, R5, and the link register to the stack. POP {R4, R5, PC} restores R4, R5, and loads LR back into PC (which returns from the subroutine).

ARM’s PUSH and POP are actually aliases for the more general STMFD SP!, {registers} (Store Multiple, Full Descending) and LDMFD SP!, {registers} (Load Multiple, Full Descending).

Memory Layout

A typical ARM program’s memory is organised into segments:

  • Text segment (.text): The program’s machine code instructions. Typically read-only. Starts at a low address.
  • Data segment (.data): Initialised global and static variables. Defined using directives in the .data section.
  • BSS segment (.bss): Uninitialised global/static variables. Zero-filled by the OS loader at startup.
  • Stack: Starts at a high address and grows toward lower addresses. Used for local variables, saved registers, and return addresses.
  • Heap: Used for dynamic memory allocation (via system calls in assembly, or malloc in C). Located between BSS and stack.

Data Definition Directives

In the .data section, you define variables using assembler directives:

  • .word N — defines a 32-bit word with value N. Example: myVar: .word 42
  • .byte N — defines a single byte.
  • .hword N — defines a 16-bit halfword.
  • .ascii ’text’ — defines a string of bytes (no null terminator).
  • .asciz ’text’ — defines a null-terminated string.
  • .space N — reserves N bytes of uninitialised space.

To load the address of a label into a register, use the LDR pseudo-instruction: LDR R0, =myVar. The assembler converts this to a PC-relative load from a literal pool.

exercises.title: LDR Instruction
options: Loads the value at memory address R5 into R2 then adds 12 to R5
Loads the word at address (R5 + 12) into R2
exercises.answer: Loads the word at address (R5 + 12) into R2

exercises.title: Pre-indexed Addressing
exercises.prompt: What distinguishes pre-indexed addressing mode [Rn, #offset]! from offset mode [Rn, #offset]?
options: It updates the base register Rn after the access
It accesses a byte instead of a word
It is faster but less accurate
exercises.answer: It updates the base register Rn after the access

exercises.title: Stack Growth Direction
exercises.prompt: In which direction does the ARM stack grow in memory?

exercises.title: LDRB vs LDR
exercises.prompt: What is the difference between LDRB and LDR instructions?
options: LDRB loads from a different memory segment
LDRB loads a single byte and zero-extends it to 32 bits
LDRB stores a byte while LDR stores a word
exercises.answer: LDRB loads a single byte and zero-extends it to 32 bits
